metadata.created_from: abstracted local Reading OCR sample patterns
metadata.purpose: Generate original TOEIC-style Reading questions from source-inspired structures without storing or paraphrasing the source questions.

allowed: Use only abstract exam structure, tested skills, document types, question counts, and distractor styles.
Create new names, companies, products, dates, locations, numbers, and business facts.
Keep the official Reading sequence concept: Part 5 independent sentence completion, Part 6 four-blank passages, Part 7 grouped reading comprehension.
forbidden: Do not copy, translate, paraphrase, or preserve source item wording.
Do not reuse source names, companies, dates, order numbers, article facts, passages, answer choices, or explanations.
Do not reconstruct OCR pages or preserve source passage structure closely enough to identify the source.

Part 5: Independent business sentence with one blank and four choices.
Grammar mix includes word form, tense, passive voice, pronouns, relative clauses, conjunctions, prepositions, collocations, and business vocabulary.
Distractors should be same-root word forms, wrong tense/aspect, wrong connector logic, wrong preposition, or plausible vocabulary with incorrect context.
Part 6: One business text with four blanks: email, memo, notice, advertisement, or business letter.
Each four-question passage should test vocabulary in context, grammar/verb form, transition logic, and sentence insertion or paragraph flow.
The passage should be natural business English with a single coherent purpose and no OCR-like artifacts.
Part 7: Single passages include email, memo, advertisement, notice, article, schedule, invoice, review, or form-like business document.
Double passages usually combine related documents such as advertisement plus inquiry, notice plus email, order confirmation plus complaint, or schedule plus memo.
Triple passages usually require cross-text reasoning across an initial document, a follow-up message, and a resolution or external update.
Questions should mix main idea, detail, inference, vocabulary-in-context, sentence insertion, and cross-text reference where applicable.
